point.title: “It was an adventure” : reflecting on unintended positive consequences of internment experience
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Rosie the Riveter World War II Home Front

record.description: Norman Hirose was born in Oakland, California and moved to Berkeley with his family in 1936. He discusses how his parents, both from the Fukuoka Prefecture in Japan, met and married in the United States. He describes his father’s occupations during and after the Great Depression. Before the start of the war, he attended school while watching the construction of the Golden Gate Bridge, Bay Bridge, and Treasure Island. Following Pearl Harbor and Executive Order 9066, his family was taken to the First Congressional Church in Berkeley, then to Tanforan Relocation Center, and finally to the Topaz incarceration site in Utah. At Tanforan, he attended a makeshift high school organized by incarcerated Bay Area college students and completed his education at Topaz High School. After the camp closed, he was sent to Santa Fe, New Mexico, with other "no-no boys" and was finally released several months thereafter. After being drafted to and serving in the Army, he attended and graduated from UC Berkeley. The interview ends with Hirose’s reflections on the overall experience of incarceration.
record.transcript:
REDMAN: My name is Sam Redman. Today is August 30, 2012, and I'm sitting down today with Norman Hirose in Berkeley, California. We're actually on campus today at the offices of the Regional Oral History Office. The very first question, Norman, is simple enough. Could you just state and spell your full name for me?
HIROSE: My name is Norman Iwao Hirose. N-O-R-M-A-N. I-W-A-O. H-I-R-O-S-E.
REDMAN: The next question is, where were you born, Norman?
HIROSE: I was born right here in Oakland, California.
REDMAN: Oakland, California. And what year were you born in?
HIROSE: That was 1926. And if you calculate it out, I am eighty-six years old.
REDMAN: Well, you look in terrific shape! If I could look like that at eighty-six, I'd be totally happy. Tell me a little about who your parents were and how they ended up in Oakland. Were they from Oakland?
HIROSE: Oh, no, no! Both my parents were from Japan. Of course, they have since passed on. But dad came from Fukuoka, a prefecture in Japan which is way down in the island of Kyushu and he came to Seattle, I think, in 1916 or somewhere around there. Just before World War I. His history from there on is quite a ways to go. I was born ten years after he got here. Mom was born also in Fukuoka Prefecture in a place very close by where my father was born. But she was born much later. She was born in 1906, at the same time of the San Francisco big fire, you know? And she came to the US just before the borders were closed to all Asians. She came here in 1923.
REDMAN: So they met in the United States? In California?
HIROSE: Oh yeah. They met in California.
REDMAN: Did you ever get the story of how they met?
HIROSE: Oh, yes! I know all about that, too.
REDMAN: Can you tell me a little about that? That'd be great.
HIROSE: Well, first of all, my father's older brother was in California. He was farming, and he wanted some help. So he called his younger brother, which was my father, and he came to Seattle and he had worked all the way from Washington down to the Sacramento Valley. And in a place called Coloma? Is that up in the valley? North of Sacramento and a little bit west. They leased land and grew rice. During World War I, they and two other friends, there were four friends. four guys got together and my uncle was married, had two sons. No, he had three sons. Anyhow, dad was not married, and then they had two other guys that joined them. They leased a huge amount of land and grew rice. And they made a great big fortune. In that time, there fortune was something. But, they kept plowing all the money they made in one year into the next year. Or the next season. And they made more money. They kept doing this for two or three times. Then, I think it was about 1918, just when the war ended, there was a big rainstorm that just flooded them out. And they lost everything they had. All four guys said, "Well, we're not going to find any work here in the farmland." So they drifted on down to the Bay Area and found all kinds of jobs. That's what happened there. That's my father's story.
REDMAN: And also his name? Could you tell me his first name?
HIROSE: My father's name is very strange. When he landed at Seattle, he called himself -- he didn't have an English name -- but he called himself Thomas. "My name is Thomas Hirose." And then he got to the Bay Area, and for some reason or another, his name changed, and he became Harvey. So he's Harvey Yonezou Hirose. Now, his Japanese name, of course, stayed the same all the time. His passport says that. It doesn't even mention the fact that he had an English name. That's something that he gave himself.
REDMAN: My assumption is that there is some pressure for people, for new immigrants to take on an English name. And of course here on the Ellis Island example with Eastern European individuals with them just changing the name at the Immigration Station. But with your dad, the fact that he changed his name again seems like there's -- was that part of his presentation of who he was?
HIROSE: I think so. I don't know his childhood very much. I know he went to school a lot. And he liked -- after he passed on, I was looking through all of his belongings, and I found a volume of The Complete Works of Shakespeare in Japanese. I said, "How can this be? He can't possibly have read all of that." Then I said, "Well, why not? Maybe he did. I don't know." [laughs] But if he read that, well I guess his understanding of English names would be more than just Tom and Thomas and Bill and William.
REDMAN: Tell me about your mother.
HIROSE: Now, my mom is a little bit different story. Her father left Japan much earlier with his second wife. He and his second wife had, I forget, six children. And they lived -- he lived -- I'm trying to get it straight now. Modesto. In Modesto, he had some sort of a, I think it was a restaurant business or a bar, a bar-restaurant, in a not too classy neighborhood, just sort of on the outskirts somewhere, where laborers went. And my grandfather called his daughter, my mother, to the US, because he said "The borders are going to close." And so she came. Well, she didn't want to, but she came anyhow. After she came, at the same time, my father moved from Coloma to the Bay Area. Of course, the Japanese people sort of got to know each other, and they knew who was living where, who was single, who had an eligible daughter for marriage, and so on and so on. A mutual friend introduced the two to each other with the approval of my grandfather and my uncle. And that's how my father and mother got married. So theirs was an arranged marriage. But if you looked at the map in Japan, they don't live far apart. They live pretty close together.
REDMAN: So it's funny that they find each other in California.
HIROSE: Well, the mutual friend is also a part of this small area in Fukuoka Prefecture in Japan. So I guess that's how it all kind of melded together.
REDMAN: I'm going to ask you a question. You would have been too young, I imagine, to really remember, but could you sort of describe for someone who doesn't know how the Japanese community changed over time. The context you were born into was before the Depression. And there was certainly a lot of Japanese Americans and Japanese Nationals working in agriculture. But they were also, many like your father, were probably also doing other things in other sorts of areas. You mentioned how your parents were setup was through this community of people who knew where the other single Japanese people were. Tell me a little bit more about that community that you were born into as you understood it.
HIROSE: Well, I don't really know it because I was born in 1926, and they got married I think the year before I was born in '25. Or '24. I think 1924 is when they got married. Anyhow, the Japanese community at that time was mostly people that came from all over Japan, here and there. And each of those community, they sort of drifted and got together again. For example, if people from the United States went to Australia or something like that, well the Wisconsin people would all sort of migrate and get together. The people from Minnesota would get to know each other. That's what happened with the Japanese community. That feeling of statehood or community-hood was probably much stronger with the Japanese people. That's why, even to this day, there are prefectural, which is the name that means state, organizations like Fukuoka Prefectural Association and Hiroshima Prefectural Association. They still exist. Every once in a while, I get a notice from the Prefectural club for Fukuoka.
REDMAN: So it's still active?
HIROSE: Still active.
REDMAN: So then you would have been a very young boy when the stock market crashes in 1929.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. In '29.
REDMAN: Four or five years old, right? So, a lot of people have told me that the crash really makes things so much worse. But others have said, "Well, you know, times weren't so great, even before." But I'm wondering if even at a young age, you came here to consciousness in the context of some really hard times in the United States. Can you talk a little bit about how the Great Depression may or may not have affected your family?
HIROSE: Well, my dad got -- the hardest thing was for him to find a job because there was no job as such. So he started his own little enterprise of being a gardener. He went around to areas in Oakland, mostly in the Piedmont area, and he got a job as a gardener for that household, or this household, or that household. So he was, in one sense, very fortunate that he was able to have clients like that. But of course, he only had a few of them. The time that he was not working was doing anything. Well, he did whatever he could do to scrape together a couple dollars for the day, you know.
REDMAN: What language was spoken at home? Was it mostly Japanese?
HIROSE: It was Japanese, all the time. I went to kindergarten at the Piedmont elementary school and I spoke Japanese. That was it, you know. Here I was, five years old, or six years old, or something like that. That was the language. So I had to stay in kindergarten two years. The first year was to learn English [laughs] And when I got so that I could talk to my playmates at least. But as far as I remember, I was always happy go lucky and went to school. I didn't know what the teacher said to me, but I don't know, I guess it was okay.
REDMAN: That was going to be my next question. I can imagine a couple of things going on there. One, it could be a really traumatic thing for a kid not to know the language. On the other hand, kids tend to be resilient. They tend to roll with things like that. They tend to learn languages really quickly. Was that your experience? You were just happy to be around other kids?
HIROSE: My experience was, as far as I know, I didn't go home crying. We lived on the other side of the street, so my mom came down to pick me up, which is across the street on Piedmont Avenue. There's a traffic signal there right in front of the school. That traffic signal's still there, too! [laughs] Anyhow, I don't think I ever cried. She never saw me crying, so I guess things went along pretty well at school. I think it was just fun playing with the other kids my own age. They would speak to me in English and I guess, gradually I would just pick up the English language and that was it.
REDMAN: That's pretty amazing. We mentioned a couple things pertinent to immigration that I think are important to go over. There's this whole long complex history, you might say, of immigration laws in the United States. In this era, there were anti-miscegenation laws that prevented non-whites from marrying whites. There was something called the Oriental Exclusion Act which barred citizenship. And then immigration quotas and attempts to find dual citizenship. It seems like your family was cognizant of these changes as they were happening. And the Japanese community might have been very aware.
HIROSE: Oh, yes. The Japanese community was aware of these because apparently, during that period just before 1924 or whenever it was, a lot of Japanese girls came to the United States. They said, "Well, all these guys are around here and they have no wives!" [laughs]
REDMAN: Like many immigrant populations, it was the men that led the way in that sense.
HIROSE: Men led the way because they had to work and get some money together. Then they would call their friends and then they would call the wives over. But all of this was going to stop. And after --
REDMAN: So that was that wave of --
HIROSE: So there was a lot of people, young people, that came during the early twenties.
REDMAN: Jumping ahead a little, when you were about ten, eleven, twelve, just a few years up to the war, in 1935, 1936, 1937, and again in 1939, the US passes neutrality law, which in essence, the US is trying to stay out of the war in this moment. Do you recall some of those tensions as building up? Or was that just something you were just unaware of?
HIROSE: I was too young or too naïve to even wonder about government. I didn't know what government was. I knew school had something to do with government. But beyond that, if a policeman was on the road, you obeyed the policeman. [laughs]
REDMAN: And that's about it.
HIROSE: That's about it! He was the only person I was afraid of or had to pay attention to. Other than that, all of the other people --
REDMAN: How about Franklin Delano Roosevelt, FDR? A lot of Americans had strong feelings one way or another towards him. In California, he was very popular for many people. It seems like your parents may not have been at home listening to fireside chats, not just because of the language barrier, but maybe not too much of an interest in politics?
HIROSE: In our family, we didn't have a lot of politics. Of course, I didn't know too much anyhow. I think my dad was aware of what is happening in the way of laws and so on, what kinds of things are happening. He was, I just can say, aware of the political things that were happening. That's about all.
REDMAN: Can you tell me back in those days when you were a young boy, a little bit about your parents' personalities? What they were like as people?
HIROSE: Oh, I don't know [laughs] The only thing I remember is that I got a whipping once, and my dad hit me. But it was only just once in my whole life. I felt it was so unfair. But that's all.
REDMAN: Do you recall the circumstances?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah! He wanted me to pile some wood up. We piled wood for winter. This pile of stuff that was out there had to be neatly piled so that it would get ready for winter and not get wet, so I had to put in the basement of the house that we rented. I was over here working on my bicycle. At least, I thought I was working on my bicycle. I had it all apart and I was going to grease it up and put it all together, which is fine. I don't know; I just didn't move fast enough. I wanted to finish my bicycle and then do the woodpile. But my dad wanted me to do the woodpile. So the woodpile got done and I cried. And I went back to my bicycle. That's the only thing I know.
REDMAN: And how about your mom?
HIROSE: She was fine. She said do this or do that. And we did. There were four of us. When we moved from Oakland to Berkeley, there were four of us and --
REDMAN: Do you recall what year you moved to Berkeley?
HIROSE: Sure. We moved right after the Depression, which was about '36, 1936 or thereabouts.
REDMAN: And do you recall what neighborhood you lived in in Berkeley in '36?
HIROSE: I can go down there right now. In fact, the other day my brother and I said, "Let's go by our old house" and we did and it's still there.
REDMAN: That's great. What street is it on?
HIROSE: It's on Woolsey Street.
REDMAN: Okay. Whereabouts?
HIROSE: Do you know where Adeline is?
REDMAN: Yes, I do actually.
HIROSE: Woolsey and Adeline. On the corner there, there used to be what was known as the South Berkeley Branch Library. But that library was sold, and there's a church there now. I think it's a Baptist church now.
REDMAN: So first, let me ask about your siblings. There are four of you. Which one are you of the four?
HIROSE: I'm the oldest. I was born in '26.
REDMAN: And then you have three younger siblings.
HIROSE: Yes. Then my sister is two years later and my brother is two years after that and my little sister two years after that.
REDMAN: And what are their first names?
HIROSE: The older of my two sister's name is Lillian. And my brother's name is Clarence. He never goes by that because he didn't like the name; he goes by his Japanese name, Sei. S-E-I. And then my younger sister, she passed away already, but her name was Alma.
REDMAN: Tell me about, then, let's say in 1936, the neighborhood you moved into in Berkeley. Could you just describe what it was like in 1936? You would have been a young boy. I'm wondering, were there other Japanese American families living in that neighborhood?
HIROSE: In that neighborhood? Not real close by. But, I guess so. About five blocks away there were other Japanese families here and there. I went down that way. I'm recalling my childhood area. That area down there, there was quite a few Japanese Americans --
REDMAN: But from what I'm getting from you, it was a pretty mixed area.
HIROSE: Oh, it's a mixed area. When I went around the corner there was our street gang after school. We played baseball in the street. We played football in the street. We played kickball in the street. Everything.
REDMAN: So those kids were from different backgrounds.
HIROSE: Oh, yes.
REDMAN: It seems to me often that little kids don't have many of the same prejudices that adults --
HIROSE: No, there was none. None that I could pick up. We never called each other anything, except we used street language. [laughs]
REDMAN: What kind of street language?
HIROSE: Well, I was referring to racial street language. Black kids were called, with "n" -- because there were no black kids in our particular neighborhood. You had to go a little bit further down Woolsey Street. About two blocks. And then there would be my very good friend, Charles Miller. He's still alive, I think. He's at Riverside. No, Irving.
REDMAN: So, kids may have used some of those slang terms --
HIROSE: Oh, yes. We used those slang terms.
REDMAN: But your sense is that they weren't necessarily as derogatory as you might imagine.
HIROSE: That's just the name.
REDMAN: Okay. So in that era, there were different sentiments around phrases and terms?
HIROSE: Well, the school that I went to was at that time called Lincoln Elementary School. But after World War II, they changed it to Malcolm X School.
REDMAN: You seemed to like school growing up. We talked about kindergarten and learning the language. How about your time in elementary school? Was that also an enjoyable experience?
HIROSE: That was also enjoyable.
REDMAN: So you liked your teachers and friends.
HIROSE: The teachers were fine. But playing with the other kids, that was really fun. And then after school, going around the corner and getting our street gang together. After school gang. That was fun too.
REDMAN: Baseball and football and sports like that were big activities.
HIROSE: Big activities, yes. And just plain running! [laughs]
REDMAN: During the Great Depression, FDR issues a response to the Great Depression, something known as the New Deal. There's a Work's Progress Administration. WPA, CCC, NYA, and these other acronyms for jobs programs or things like that. That may not have resonated as a young boy but I want to make sure to ask were those New Deal programs?
HIROSE: We all knew that WPA meant a government job. For people who couldn't find a job, they could go there and work. They build lots of things. Mainly, the school buildings in Berkeley, a lot of them were renovated during WPA. I think our school was renovated. Longfellow was -- they fixed that portion of it up and that kind of stuff.
REDMAN: Again, a kid might have had some vague understanding of what that program was and it would have been hard for them to ignore if their school was being renovated, of course.
HIROSE: Sure. There's big signs out there saying "This is a WPA Project." Well, we thought, "Oh, the government is coming here, and they're going to fix our school." By government, I thought City of Berkeley. But it wasn't. It was the federal government. But anyhow, that happened. And then also, I remember I always thought that -- of course, it happened during late '37, '38, '39, on Ashby Avenue trucks kept going back and forth and back and forth on that street, down to the Bay full of dirt. Then they come back empty and they go back down the Bay full of dirt. But what they were doing is they were going across the bridge making Treasure Island. That went on for a long time. I think, fourth grade, fifth grade, and sixth grade.
REDMAN: That was a lot of earth that they had to move.
HIROSE: Yes, that was a lot of dirt that had to be moved out there.
REDMAN: Let me ask about two things that were completed around the same time during your childhood. In '36, the Bay Bridge opens and in 1937 the Golden Gate Bridge opens. Do you recall those things in the Bay Area and being impressed by that? What was your reaction?
HIROSE: Oh, yes. By the time I was in sixth and seventh grade, I had a bicycle. My dad bought me a bicycle, so I went up to the Berkeley hills, and we looked around the Bay, and we saw them building these bridges. The Golden Gate Bridge was way over there and the towers went up, all the cables went up, and so on. We didn't up to the hills every day, but every once in a while, two or three of us would spend Saturday going up to the Hills to look down at the Bay. So I remember that. The Bay Bridge was built. But the Bay Bridge was a very interesting thing. It opened in '39, I think it was, and at the same time, Treasure Island was finished, and they were going to have the World's Fair there. So my dad took us to the World's Fair. He put is in the car and we went. But everybody on the Bridge was stopped, parked right along the edge. The top deck of the Bay Bridge went two ways. One to San Francisco and from San Francisco to the Bay Bridge. But all the people on both sides of the bridge, along the edge, they got to stop, [laughs] and get out of the cars and look over the railing and see what it was like. During the daytime, that's what we did.
REDMAN: So you went to the World's Fair in '39
HIROSE: Yes, I did.
REDMAN: What do you recall about that? What do you remember about that?
HIROSE: I remember -- who's the swimmer?
REDMAN: Oh. Jack --
HIROSE: Lady swimmer.
REDMAN: No, don't recall that name.
HIROSE: Rose. No. I don't remember her name. But anyhow, I asked my mother, and I begged her to let me see this show. Every little place you went cost money. She said, "No, we can't go there. That's too expensive." But I said, "That's all I want to see. I won't see anything else." So she let me go. I don't know where they got their money. I really don't. It just amazes me. I think we four were really spoiled, I don't know why. Anyhow, I did see the swimming. That was the most memorable thing. I even remember to this day -- of course, the other exhibits were nice. I remember we went to some science thing, and they had a kitchen and a kitchen stove. And the man had his hand on top of the kitchen stove. He says, "See, it's not hot. It's not hot! I don't burn my hand." Then he would get out a pot of water and put it right on top of the stove. In a few seconds, the water was boiling.
REDMAN: So these technological innovations that, as a child --
HIROSE: Just magic. Just magic. Then after he boiled his egg or whatever it that he was he was boiling, he took the pot off the stove, opened the lid like this, he reached his hand inside of the pot, and pulled out a popsicle. [laughs] And it was frozen! [laughs] I said, "How can that be!"
REDMAN: [laughs] That's so funny. So things like that captured your attention. A lot of historians have written about that Treasure Island Fair in '39 and that one of the themes that they wanted to project for the fair was that they wanted to cast San Francisco as a gateway to the Pacific. But at the same time, there's an embargo and boycott of Japanese businesses and goods following the start of the second Sino-Japanese War. There's something that forms on this campus called the Campus Committee for Non-Participation in Japanese Aggression. So there's already by this time stirring up of tensions in the Pacific. But I wonder, to a small boy, if any of that would have been clear.
HIROSE: No. They weren't nice to each other; I knew that. I didn't know what or why, and I'm sure it was the economics of the time that caused all this but that's just over my head. [laughs]
REDMAN: I'll jump forward just a few years from '39. The next question is about December 7th, 1941, the day Pearl Harbor was attacked. What do you recall about that, what do you remember?
HIROSE: Oh, yes, vividly. I finished elementary school. Seventh, eighth, and ninth grade were at Willard Junior High School which is on Telegraph, and I went there. And then the summer of 1941 is when I graduated from the ninth grade. Then in the fall, I came to Berkeley High School.
REDMAN: So that would have been your first semester --
HIROSE: First semester of Berkeley High. And that was the only semester. Because in December of '41, when Pearl Harbor was bombed, I was in the tenth grade at Berkeley High School. They tore it down, recently, just last week, but the gym was the auditorium. And the Berkeley Community Theater, the big round building, wasn't there. They were building it then. They stopped building it during the war. On Monday following Pearl Harbor, the principal, Mr. Latande, called the school to the auditorium and said, "We are going to listen to radio because the President of the United States has a message for us." And that's when Roosevelt declared -- we were all there assembled -- declared war on the Axis Powers.
REDMAN: Can you think back to being in that assembly at Berkeley High School? What was that like for you?
HIROSE: First, it was quiet after the declaration. Nobody clapped, nobody said "rah" or "boo" or anything. It was so quiet. Then the principal got up and said, "All right, it's over now. Go back to your rooms." So we all left. But it was quiet, very, very quiet. I looked around and looked around, but nothing.
REDMAN: So you don't recall the Japanese students at Berkeley High School getting a particular reaction at that time?
HIROSE: I didn't think so. In fact, no one said anything. That's the strange part of it. It was so quiet. Can you imagine? I think there were pretty close to 3,000 kids. Then, after we got up and we started moving out, then kids started to whisper to each other. Then we got out of the gym, there was some noise. I mean, what do you expect?
REDMAN: But you remember that silence?
HIROSE: But that silence there that -- actually, that silence was something, I think. The behavior was -- Then I thought, "Well, I'll go through school," and I went through school that day and I went home. And on my way home, I walked from Berkeley High back to my home. My mother wouldn't let us use the bicycle, she said, "You walk to school." That was that. So we walked home. And I thought the next day I was going to really be in trouble. Maybe some kids would gang up on me or whatever might happen.
REDMAN: You were afraid at that --
HIROSE: Afraid at the moment. At that moment. I walked to school just like I did every other Tuesday or Wednesday and nothing happened. I went to school and said, "Hi, Tam!" and "Hi, Chuck!" and so on. And we just went through the day. The days just went on.
REDMAN: Between December and February.
HIROSE: Yes, all the way. Then some time during -- then the evacuation occurred the following year.
REDMAN: I understand that the Executive Order 9066 was issued on February 19, 1942. So I'm asking you to recall a very small window in this next question, and you talked about it a little bit. I'm just going to ask you this follow up and ask you to go on a little bit. Can you tell me a little bit more about what might have been going through your mind between December and February when the evacuation order was issued?
HIROSE: Before the evacuation, I just went to school. I said, "Well, I guess we're just going to keep going to school and just go on like it's been that way for years. Centuries!" [laughs]
REDMAN: So you didn't think that there would be --
HIROSE: I didn't think there were going to be any such things. Then there was a Japanese newspaper -- there were two Japanese newspapers in the Bay Area. Of course, Roosevelt's Order was printed in there, of course. And my folks knew about it.
REDMAN: What was their reaction?
HIROSE: The said, "Oh, my goodness! What shall we do?" We didn't know what to do. We're Buddhist, so we went to the Buddhist Temple. I didn't go. My folks went there. They all decided they were going to -- "What are we going to do? We're going to have to do this, this, this, and this," which meant, you have to get rid of all your stuff because the only things you could carry were two suitcases. Every person was allowed that much stuff. Everything had to be carried by the people in the family. My little sisters, they were too small. They could carry small things, but that's about all. Mostly, they just got their clothing. All we really had were dishes to eat with, utensil, toilet articles, and clothing. And that's it. So what are we going to do with all the rest of the stuff we had? All the furniture in the house, you had to get rid of it. Get rid of it by the time we have to go. We got rid of our furniture. My father sold most of the furniture. If anything was left over, whoever came in got it. Like I said, we had rented the property. My dad sold his two -- he had two cars and he sold one and the other one -- a man said he couldn't give you any money but he towed it away.
REDMAN: It seems like that would have been a demoralizing thing.
HIROSE: Yeah! The people that were in business? I don't know what they did. But they got someone to take care of their stuff while they were gone. I know of two stories on that one. Somebody had -- I think it was a grocery store guy. His grocery store he got back when he came back after the evacuation. The nursery man, he had a nursery. And when he came back the guy didn't let him have his nursery back. So they went and started some other business.
REDMAN: When your material possessions as such are being taken, did you get a sense from either your father or your mother of any anger at that?
HIROSE: No, they weren't angry. They were just trying to get whatever they could. I think mainly they tried to get as much money as they could because they knew that -- well, you had to carry everything your suitcase. Or two suitcases, that's it. You had to carry it. So the best thing to carry would be money. Even though we didn't know what we would do with it once we got to our camp. You couldn't take your car and all that kind of stuff with you. Also, no cameras are allowed. They said, "No cameras, no radios"; I think that was it. No cameras and radios. This was all before TV and before iPads and all these other things, cell phones and all that kind of stuff.
REDMAN: You mentioned the Japanese language newspapers. It seems like that would be pretty important for your parents in terms of getting their news and staying connected to their community. You mentioned that there were two Japanese newspapers. Were they subscribed to both of them?
HIROSE: No.
REDMAN: Did they have one over the other, and why?
HIROSE: No. My father subscribed to the Nichi Bei Times. The other was called Hokobei Mainichi . Some people subscribed to that. Some people subscribed to the Nichi Bei. I don't know how they chose which one to subscribe to.
REDMAN: They picked one, okay. That's great. There's a group that starts gaining traction and gaining steam in California that's initially called the Japanese Committee on Fair Play, and it becomes the Japanese American Citizens League. There's also conflicting forces going on. There's also the Native Sons and Daughters of the California West. The American Legion is lobbying. The efforts of Earl Warren, the efforts of the Attorney General of California and the state government who helps facilitate in many, many ways the removal of Japanese to camps. Were you aware of any of those other organizations, or do you think your family -- ? It seems like that might be something you might not have been aware of at the time.
HIROSE: After we came back, I heard about the JACL, the Japanese American Citizens League. But I don't think I heard of it before then. But I did know about the prefectural clubs. They were formed many, many years before. And they had little meetings and gatherings. They had picnics and stuff. All the fun things we went to. [laughs]
REDMAN: So that's a memory. Your father was not a citizen at that time. In terms of property, in terms of the treatment between citizens and non-citizens, it seems, maybe part of the calculus of your father in going with cash over these possessions might be his citizen status in terms of strategizing what he's going to do and bring to the camp. I'm just curious how he made those decisions. It seems a horrible thing to have to decide what to take.
HIROSE: Yes, he had to decide that. Fortunately, he didn't own any property. He didn't own it because he couldn't. And so what do you do? Well then I guess cash was the only thing you could have. I think what he did was he had a bank account, and I think that's where he put it. That would probably be the safest place to put his money.
REDMAN: Let's talk about terminology. Historians have debated and there are many personal opinions on the terms used to describe the camp experience. First, the term "assembly centers" was the term used to describe the between-stop that sometimes prolonged between stops for Japanese. "Internment camp," "relocation camp," and "concentration camp" have been used as terms. Can you tell me how you feel about those terms?
HIROSE: The assembly centers, those camps were in mostly racetracks in California.
REDMAN: Tanforan is one of the famous examples. What about -- ?
HIROSE: That's where I went. I went to Tanforan. On the day that -- I don't know how they decided who was going to go on which day, but anyhow every family had a date on which they were to report to the Congregational Church [First Congressional Church in Berkeley, CA].
REDMAN: If you could tell me even the mechanisms for how you, then, were shipped. By train, by --
HIROSE: Oh, yeah!
REDMAN: Go on though. Please.
HIROSE: I don't know how we got there to the church. I know we didn't walk, so somebody must have taken us there. Anyhow, we had our suitcases, and everything else at home was gone. Essentially, the cars were sold, some of the furniture was sold, my sister's grand piano was sold, some of the kitchen articles were given to our neighbor. Mr. and Mrs. Lindberg lived next door to us. We put it in boxes, and they said they'll care of it and hold it for us until we got back. Anyhow, we came to the Congregational Church over here on Dana and Durant. Everybody was there. We all got nametags. We got tagged with names and numbers. I forget what our number was. Anyhow, we had a number, our family number. And then the Greyhound buses came. They lined up. I don't know how many there were but the whole block was full of buses, so we got on there. My guess is that this happened for several days. Two days, so on and so on and so on. Then the next day -- somebody somewhere organized all of this stuff. So we got on the bus and we went over the Bay Bridge. We went to Tanforan. We got off there at the grandstands, and there was inside, underneath the grandstands, there's this big, big area. I guess that used to be where all the bettings took place. But anyhow, the big hollow, hall-like place. My dad went in there and they knew we were coming. They said you go to so-and-so place. So-and-so meaning your apartment for the night. And we were in stall number fourteen on the very end. That's all I remember.
REDMAN: These were horse stalls?
HIROSE: Yeah, the horse stalls. The regular horse stalls where the race horses used to live in, were kept in. That's where we lived.
REDMAN: What was it like when you arrived? Do you remember? The images that I've seen at Tanforan in the time that was spent there that was maybe for eight months, the transformation is astounding. You see these horse stalls. I mean, they look like racetrack horse stalls to begin with and then by the time the center closes, there are nice manicured gardens.
HIROSE: Little gardens all in front!
REDMAN: Talk about when you first arrived.
HIROSE: Well, see we arrived and we were let off at the grandstands. We had to take our stuff. My dad went someplace and got our number. It was fourteen something-something-something, meaning horse stall number fourteen. We said, "Where is it?" "Well, it's over there!" There was no way to get there except walk, so we had to walk through the infield. Now, the infield had barracks built for housing. Some people got to live in those, but we didn't. That's okay. We went through there and we went to fourteen, whatever it was, on the end. Ours was an end stall. So we went in there. And it was okay. What more can you expect? I think what they did is, they must have washed them down as much as they could. Then they whitewashed, you know, splatter whitewash all over the place. That was our room.
REDMAN: But pretty plain.
HIROSE: Oh, very plain! Since there were six of us in our family, and we got an end stall -- we actually got two stalls. This horse was this horse stall here then next to it was the horse stalls facing this way, [uses hand signals to draw layout] from the edge.
REDMAN: So they were sort of [motions with hands] --
HIROSE: Yeah, like that. My brother and I got one. My two sisters got the other one. My mom and dad were in the main room. The doors were all double-doors, you know? Dutch doors, is that what you call it?
REDMAN: Sure, for horses. So the horse can stick their head out.
HIROSE: Yes, for horses. And all they did was, they nailed them together so it made one door. That was it!
REDMAN: That was it. This is the final question I'll ask on this tape. You said being at Tanforan was an all right arrangement.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah! It was fun.
REDMAN: You had fun?
HIROSE: Yeah, we had fun. Kids had fun.
REDMAN: That's a great question! Why was that experience fun for the kids? Because to me, looking back on this experience, the first thing that you think is not that. You think about the trauma involved in that experience, and it must have been very traumatic for your parents. But for kids --
HIROSE: For my mom, she just -- organizing it just so we could go and eat -- see, everybody had to go to the grandstands to eat. That means you had to take your own dishes. You go up there and you hold it out, hold your plate out, and they would put the food in. Now the people that prepare the food were also internees anyhow, and so that's the way it was done. There was no outside labor to come in and do all this. You could eat on the bleachers or eat inside because there were tables inside, underneath the bleaches. Or you could take your food and go home, eat at home. Then you had to wash them and get your plates ready so that you could go at lunchtime.
REDMAN: How about water? I imagine there's no running water in your rooms.
HIROSE: No, there's no running water in our apartment. But at the end of each -- well, I don't know how many people. There were bathrooms and shower rooms and laundry rooms. The laundry room and the bathrooms and the toilets, were all in this one building. And they had lots of these buildings all over the place. So people, like the people in our barracks -- it was really long, almost a block long for a horse stall.
REDMAN: It seems like a lot of people.
HIROSE: There was a lot of people. Our little bathroom area was for the people that lived near it. And they used it and we had to use it. "Oh, I guess it's full now, so we'll come back later."
REDMAN: Okay, so it wasn't always perfect --
HIROSE: Oh, well! It was okay. But there was hot water, so we were able to bathe. That was good.
REDMAN: All right, my name is Sam Redman and it's August 30, 2012. This is my second tape today with Norman Hirose. When we left off, we were talking about terminology. So I'd like to get your thoughts on that. Let's return to that question. We talked about Tanforan. There's also Camp Harmony, Pomona, and Santa Anita. Do you have anything to add about those other locations? I'm just wondering if you had friends or family go to these other locations in California or was it exclusively Tanforan? Was it like everyone you know in the Japanese American community was with you at that assembly center?
HIROSE: Tanforan was for -- by the way, it's called "assembly center." The organization that was heading it is called WCCA, or Wartime Civilian Control Administration.
REDMAN: So then is it only later then that the WRA, the War Relocation Authority, are there administering the actual camps but not yet the assembly centers, from the sounds of it.
HIROSE: No, they did not have anything to do with the assembly centers.
REDMAN: That's an important point of clarification. Thank you. So then, go ahead.
HIROSE: The WRA, War Relocation Authority, they were building the camps while the WCCA was gathering the people and getting ready to transport them to the camps. And I don't know where they decided, but they decided that they were going to make ten of them, which they did. And each one was to hold about 10,000. That's what they were supposed to do. It sort of worked out that way, anyhow. Now, the other assembly centers in California were all racetracks, and I don't know who went where, but I met people from Tule Lake, which became a WRA Center anyhow. And then there were some -- it was Santa Anita?
REDMAN: Yes.
HIROSE: Yes, it was a WCCA Center but it never became a WRA Center. Like Tanforan never became a WRA Center.
REDMAN: So the idea was that those would shut down after people --
HIROSE: They were shut down after they were --
REDMAN: -- went to those permanent camps. So then I'll ask directly how do you feel about the terms "internment camps" versus "relocation camp" versus "concentration camp" in terms of the actual -- we'll talk about your life at Topaz. I want to get the story itself. But first, do you have any strong feelings about the term "concentration camp"?
HIROSE: No, I think -- I don't know where it all started from, but I somehow think it was sociologists or people who pretended to be sociologists. Or people who thought they knew something about sociology. And the people that were to their left. And they said, "We need to do something to make this experience more visible, more immediate, more something or other," so that people will understand it was a concentration camp because that has a different meaning than "relocation center." It was called "the relocation center" in the beginning because that's what the War Relocation Authority said it was going to be called. I think that was a government term.
REDMAN: It seems like "internment" is the more common description. And you've --
HIROSE: "Internment camps."
REDMAN: Yes. Would you agree with that impression?
HIROSE: Well, that's what they called them, sort of. But they called them relocation centers because we were relocated from assembly centers to relocation centers.
REDMAN: So when Japanese Americans, when you talk to other people of your generation who went through the same experience, I understand that many people simply refer to it as "camp."
HIROSE: Yeah, "camp" [laughs] "What camp did you [laughs] -- I was in Heart Mountain Camp!" We don't say "relocation center." I don't hear it very much.
REDMAN: That's interesting. We'll get back to that later. Let me ask first then. Let's return to your narrative story. After Tanforan, you were sent to Topaz.
HIROSE: Right.
REDMAN: Can you tell me about that story? The movement of your family?
HIROSE: Well, as you know, Tanforan is a horserace track, so they had railroad lines already there. So all we did is, they said "Okay, people in barracks so-and-so-and-so-and-so are going to go today." And then other people went before us. We said "Bye. See you later." Some people are going to go after us and we said, "Well, we'll see you when you get there!?" We all got on the train, carried our stuff, gathered up all that we had in our barracks, marched to where the train was, and got on the train with our bags, and then just went all the way from Tanforan straight on, right straight through.
REDMAN: What was it like to be on that train?
HIROSE: Oh, it was hot! It was warm for me. It was, what was it, September, October? That part? The rains hadn't started yet. It was before the rains. Then, of course, crossing the desert was warm.
REDMAN: It doesn't seem to bother you too much.
HIROSE: No, it didn't bother me. There was no rain. That's the only thing.
REDMAN: You had mentioned that in your recollection of the time at Tanforan and in this experience, that things weren't so tough for you psychologically. What about your siblings? Were they similarly -- ?
HIROSE: Well, I think we were always running out and playing, trying to find out where our friends live so we could find them easily. Then of course once we knew where they were, it was pretty easy to go there the second time. And then by that time, "What shall we do tomorrow?" and that was it. Mostly, it was playing sports.
REDMAN: I'll get to back that in a moment. Was it unusual for you to go from a neighborhood in Berkeley that was a pretty diverse neighborhood; you'd mentioned that there were other Japanese-American families, but there were other families in between.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. Lots of them.
REDMAN: So was it unusual for you to go into an all-Japanese community then?
HIROSE: Oh, yes. It was almost like living in Berkeley but except that everybody was Japanese so that if you wanted to, you could play with your friends next door if they were of the same age. But we all sort of stuck to age groups. I mean, the older kids didn't want to be bothered with young punks, [laughs] and little kids would play with their friends and so on. I don't know, I have never thought about that. I went looking for my friend Howard all the time. [laughs]
REDMAN: To try to make it through.
HIROSE: Yeah.
REDMAN: Much has been made of the generational differences between Japanese, Japanese Americans in this era. Issei being the first generation, then Nisei being the second generation, Sansei being the third generation, and Kibei being a term for people who were born in the United States but educated in Japan. There's some stereotypes that go along with these generations. The idea that the Issei were stoic, sort of this emotionless stoic, older generation. The Issei as loyal and the Nisei was rebellious. And the Nisei and Kibei relations might have been tense or problematic. What do you think of those inter-generational differences in the Japanese-American community? Or whether people were born Japan or born in the United States, how did that affect the dynamic, if at all?
HIROSE: You know, I think all of that was invented, personally. I guess these pretend-sociologists invented this to write a history book. I don't think that really happened that much. I don't think I had -- I had Kibei friends, and I knew he was a Kibei but so what? I didn't care. If we had friends and if we played whatever we played, it was fine.
REDMAN: So you think the portrayal of those tensions has been overblown?
HIROSE: Overblown, yes. Very much so. But, I don't want to dismiss the fact that there were indeed certain groups, whether they were Niseis or Kibeis or Sanseis or whatever, there were groups that, within, that were antagonistic toward them, I think, purely for the same of antagonism, not because they didn't like them. I'm sure that if they got to know them and -- "Well, he's a nice guy! Why should I be mad at him?" Yeah.
REDMAN: So there may have been some of it, but --
HIROSE: I think it was actually manufactured. That's my opinion. Do you know about the No-No?
REDMAN: Yes, so we can get to that.
HIROSE: The No-No People --
REDMAN: So there's a loyalty questionnaire that the US Government announces. In January 1943, I understand that they announce that they're going to create this new segregated unit for the US Military, what ultimately becomes the 442nd Regiment. In February 1943, the War Relocation Authority announces a questionnaire that includes the following questions. Here are the two controversial questions in that questionnaire. Question 27 asked, "Are you willing to serve in the Armed Forces of the United States on combat duty wherever ordered?" And Question 28, the next question, was "Will you swear unqualified allegiance to the United States and faithfully defend the United States?" And so on and so forth, and "Do you foreswear any form of allegiance to the Japanese Emperor or any other foreign government power or organization?" So I understand that phrase No-No Boys comes to describe people answering "No" to both of these questions. Would you tell me what that experience was like for you?
HIROSE: Well, for me, actually it was my mother made the decision that I would answer it "No, no" and the reason was that because she wanted to go to Japan. She wanted to go to Japan because she thought everything there would be fine. Well, it wasn't fine there. But anyhow, all of the people that were going to be sent to Japan were all taken to Tule Lake. They decided that was the best place to do it. And I think from there they went to Washington and then went across the ocean from Seattle. But anyhow, just at the time when all of this motion was going on, my father had a heart attack. The doctor says, "No, your family can't move." So in that sense, I was very lucky. We got to stay in the United States.
REDMAN: So had your father's health --
HIROSE: If he was healthy, we would have gone to Tule Lake and have gone to Japan. And I don't know what would have happened.
REDMAN: That's an amazing turning point in your life. Do you reflect back and think about that every once in a while?
HIROSE: Well, I was sorry my dad had a heart attack. Of course, he didn't die at that time. But he did have to rest. He got better and better, and he was fine when we got out in '46. When he left camp and they closed Topaz, he came home and he was fine. But wow, things would have been a lot different for me. But I think, if I can --
REDMAN: Go ahead.
HIROSE: It was during this moving back and forth that these aggressive groups of guys would -- I don't know how they got organized, but I get agitator-types are always available, and they would organize them. And the people that went to Tule Lake, some of the guys there would organize them into agitation groups. And when the people at Tule Lake that were going to be sent to other war relocations, they would yell and scream at them as being disloyal to Japan. I've heard that kind of thing happening.
REDMAN: Those are things that you've heard, but that wasn't your personal experience --
HIROSE: Well, no.
REDMAN: -- is what you're saying. Some folks have talked about the inter-generational pressure of how to answer the loyalty oath and you said your mom made the decision.
HIROSE: My mom decided that we were going to go to Japan and my father said we were going to Japan. I had to write down "No, no" so I checked off "No, no." And I was told by the counselor there, "You know that's going to happen to you, is that you're going to have to go!" I says, "Well, there isn't much I can do about it because my mom says we're going to go, so our whole family is going to stick together and go." He says, "Oh, okay."
REDMAN: Oh, but you remember that exchange with that counselor.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. The counselor was a woman counselor. I don't know her name now.
REDMAN: Was she white or Japanese?
HIROSE: No, she was a white person. She was a US Government employee as far as I can tell.
REDMAN: But she told you what the potential consequences --
HIROSE: The consequences.
REDMAN: Then the last question on this: much has been made of the concept of the No-No Boys but especially those who were of military age. I suspect you were even a little young in '43 to join the Armed Services. I'm basically asking you what the young men who were maybe a little older than you. People have talked about some of the emotional pressures there of -- if you join the 442nd, how can you possibly, for some, how can you possibly join a military that's fighting for a country that's denying your rights? On the other hand, there were other pressures and feelings and sentiments of loyalty? What do you think those men went through?
HIROSE: I don't know because I was just a year too young or two years too young. In '43, I would be, what, fifteen years old. And I wasn't yet registered for the draft. But I was just about ready to register for the draft. I said, "Wow, they must have been -- each individual must have been somewhat upset about "What shall I do? What shall I do?"
REDMAN: So it definitely was a tough --
HIROSE: It was a tough time for them. Well, when it was my turn to be drafted, of course, I got drafted in '46, '47, which is four years later, and it was not at all problematic with me. I just said, "Oh, I'm going to go! They said I have to go, I go. Get it done, and get it over with."
REDMAN: I want to talk a little bit, if it's all right, about your life at Topaz. The first thing I want to ask about is something that came up a couple times is sports. Can you tell me about baseball at the camp?
HIROSE: Well, first of all, in camp, we had Topaz High School. We did have a high school. It was a pretty nice high school. We organized varsity sports. Well, varsity as varsities go. Anyhow, we had sports: football, basketball, and baseball, and track. We arranged with schools in the area. Fillmore is one and Delta is the other one. There's two schools where the kids there --
REDMAN: Outside?
HIROSE: Outside. Since we couldn't go out, they came to us. Next to our high school was this big block. Actually, it was four blocks where they didn't build any houses, and that became our athletic field. It was huge.
REDMAN: So lots of space?
HIROSE: Oh, lots of space.
REDMAN: And different in the Bay Area in some respects.
HIROSE: Well the only thing is that it was all sand. Not sand, it was sort of muddy. Anyhow. In the wintertime it was muddy.
REDMAN: Not great infield or outfield for playing baseball. Were you playing any sports?
HIROSE: I was on the track team.
REDMAN: Do you remember what sorts of things you did in track?
HIROSE: I did running. I ran the short dashes. We had fifty-yard and hundred-yard and of course, there was a long one. I didn't want to get in the long ones, personally.
REDMAN: I'll ask about school in just a moment, but do you think that the sports programs helped those young people have some sense of normalcy in the camps?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. Definitely. Yeah.
REDMAN: It seems like that's kind of a bright spot in terms of the experiences that people have.
HIROSE: Well, I think that because we had sports groups and we had fairly decent coaches, and we did have some skill in that area. In football we were too small, so we always lost. Well, lost most of the time. Baseball, we could pretty much hold our own pretty good. In track, we could hold our own most of the time. It's sort of come-and-go. Like, pole-vaulting we didn't know to do that. We just didn't.
REDMAN: But those seem like happy memories.
HIROSE: Yeah, those are happy things. But around each one, like in football season, the girls organized their cheerleading squad. "What are they doing?" "They're our cheerleaders." "Oh? Okay!" [laughs] I think they had a lot of fun. "What kind of uniforms are we going to have? What kind of skirts shall we wear?" "What kind of shoes are we going to wear?" and so on and on and on. But anyhow, they did get uniforms on. So the times, like if it's football season or basketball season or baseball season, we gathered around that event.
REDMAN: Let's talk about school. What do you remember about going to school at Tule Lake?
HIROSE: Oh, going to school was fine. Can I go back to the assembly center?
REDMAN: Sure.
HIROSE: Okay, at the assembly center, we were there roughly March of '43, no, '42. Sorry. And we were there for about six or seven months, eight months maybe. And people that were in turn from college age, somehow or another, they got together and says, "We got to teach these kids. They can't play all day; they've got to go to school." And so they had, underneath the bleachers, school. And they taught math and English. I think that was about it. I know I was in a math class. I know I was in an English class.
REDMAN: And these were college kids [as teachers]?
HIROSE: Yes.
REDMAN: Do you remember what school? Were they Bay Area schools?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. Not all of them, but most of them are from Cal. Our group that went to Tanforan is the Bay Area. And they weren't -- well, the schools, the colleges that kids went to around the Bay Area were the public schools. So Cal and some came from St. Mary's, I think, but I don't know. But not too many from Stanford because that's too expensive. San Francisco State.
REDMAN: From the sounds of it, maybe there won't be much to expand on, but I understand that at Topaz, as with many other of the camps, that those early educational programs that you were describing at Tanforan, they start to become enriched. There are more types of classes. I understand that at some camps there were pretty robust art programs.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah!
REDMAN: So can you tell me a little more about school at Tule Lake once you arrive?
HIROSE: Well, we went to Topaz. That's where I went.
REDMAN: Sorry, sorry. Topaz.
HIROSE: It's okay. At Tanforan, the courses were skimpy, you know, just English and math. There might have been some social studies of some kind or other. Then we went to Topaz and at Topaz, the administrative offices of the camp actually organized a school. From outside, they hired a principal, assistant principal, counselor, and several teachers. But not enough. The rest of them -- the college kids were plugged in wherever they needed to be. So we had a big program; we had a nice program, I thought. We had everything.
REDMAN: In terms of the educational quality of Berkeley High School, it seems like there would be a lot of opportunities for your education to fall behind in terms of being sent away to a camp for the duration of the war. But the Japanese community makes all these strides in building these schools and implementing these programs. From your point of view, it seems to have been pretty successful.
HIROSE: I think it was really quite successful. Oh, we didn't have any fancy courses like woodworking or something like that. But I remember our chemistry teacher, Dr. Goodman. He's from the Bay Area. He's since passed on. He was here; he was at Topaz. Social studies teacher is Eleanor Gerard, I remember. She married and later taught at Castro Valley High School. She passed on, too.
REDMAN: How about art?
HIROSE: Art? You know, I don't know the teacher's name, but I remember there was an art teacher. A problem was supplies. For some reason, I don't know, but this is what I remember, instead of having nice white paper, he got construction paper. Red, blue, green, yellow, and all that. He said, "What are we going to do with all of this?" He had tons of it. And they said, "That's okay, just give it to the elementary school kids. They can use it." So they passed it there. I don't know what he did, but he did have a very good Art program, I thought.
REDMAN: But supplies --
HIROSE: I mean, not a lot. Drawing, painting a little bit, but mostly just drawing. So I guess that would be it.
REDMAN: One of the topics that have come up a lot in terms of education is the way civics and Americanism and patriotism was taught. Can you speak to that at all in the school settings?
HIROSE: Yes. Of course, I was in the eleventh and twelfth grades there, at Topaz, and we had to study civics, and we did, and US government.
REDMAN: Did that seem strange or was that pretty natural?
HIROSE: Well, this is the way it is, so I said "This is the way it is, so I guess that's what I better study." So we did! But there wasn't any "what are we studying this for when it doesn't mean anything?" I don't think there was that attitude among the students.
REDMAN: So the adults may have had a different perception?
HIROSE: The adults may have had that kind of a perception.
REDMAN: But for a young kid, that sort of critical --
HIROSE: That's the way it was done.
REDMAN: Can we talk about the availability of clothing, food, or other necessary resources?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. Those who were employed got eight dollars a month. Professional people, like the doctor at the clinic, got sixteen dollars a month. I don't know what they got, I don't know if they had extra money or anything.
REDMAN: Do you recall some of the other jobs that people could have had?
HIROSE: Yes. Well, my dad, he worked on a pig farm. He was a regular laborer, so he got eight dollars a month. I don't know what happened to his money, but I know he got it. But I guess my mother took care of it, so that was that.
REDMAN: And clothing? Was that ever an issue? You came with the clothes on your back.
HIROSE: Yes, that's about it.
REDMAN: And those had to last?
HIROSE: They had to last for a long time. As long as they could. We could buy clothes at the PX. They had an Army PX there.
REDMAN: A supply store.
HIROSE: Yes. Actually, what it was, it was Sears Roebuck, Montgomery Ward, and something else. Catalogs. They had lots of those catalogs. We go there and say, "All right, I need a pair of jeans." So I go through there, and then you compare the price in some, and says "Okay, we'll get it from Montgomery Ward this time." So we order it, pay for it.
REDMAN: So that's how --
HIROSE: And then that's how I got my pair of jeans. It just took a while to get them. Not that long.
REDMAN: There's different landscape here for different individuals in terms of their ability to leave camps, like to have a pass to go. Was that something that you witnessed or knew about, of people either having permission to leave camps or to come back? Or were people there for the entire duration?
HIROSE: Well, if you had employment outside, you could go out. Like some people went to Michigan or something, wherever. They got a job there in Michigan, doing something, working in the laundry or whatever. They got that commitment from the employer, so they could go.
REDMAN: How about the relationships between the internees and the guards? Can you talk about that?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. It was very clear. There was a barbed wire fence around the whole place. And inside the barbed wire fence was another fence. It was just a demarcation line. "Don't get any closer than this to the barbed wire fence," which was very tall and so on. So if you got to that line that was it. But then as soon as you started walking that way, the guards were watching you. That's all. But you didn't talk to them. They didn't talk to you. The only words they yelled is, "Get away from the fence!" [laughs]
REDMAN: That was it.
HIROSE: That was it.
REDMAN: Did they come to represent something to you?
HIROSE: No.
REDMAN: They were just kind of anonymous figures?
HIROSE: They were just there. What for, nobody knew. I mean, I didn't know what they were there for. Why am I going to go to the fence? He's got the rifle, not me. [laughs]
REDMAN: How about that barbed wire? Looking at that must have been a reminder.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. I said, "Okay, that's the end of my -- my area that I live in ends there right now." Of course, it was just one square mile. [Brief pause in tape]
REDMAN: I'm back with Norman. I understand that in the wintertime, there was ice-skating.
HIROSE: Yes, there was. We had ice-skating. Every block had -- one of the buildings was for recreational purposes. Outside of it, of course, some blocks said, "We're going to make a garden, or a vegetable garden."
REDMAN: Did people call them "victory gardens?"
HIROSE: No. [laughs] No. I never heard that.
REDMAN: That's -- Sorry, back to the skating. Go ahead.
HIROSE: What they did was, they just built -- it's not a levee, what do you call them? Just a little mound of dirt all around? They filled it with water. And that was it. That was the skating rink. It would be about -- well one barracks long was the longest one that I ever saw. That would be -- wow, that would be quite a bit though.
REDMAN: Did people use it?
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. The people would go there and use it all the time.
REDMAN: I've also read about apple butter and marmalade as being staples of food at the camp. But I wonder if any food jumps out to you as memorable?
HIROSE: The staple was -- we really had rice. That was the fundamental staple.
REDMAN: I wonder if rationing --
HIROSE: I don't know where apple sauce -- I never had -- did I have applesauce?
REDMAN: Apple butter?
HIROSE: Apple butter? Oh, apple butter! That's that brown stuff. Yeah, we had that often.
REDMAN: Okay. And then marmalade?
HIROSE: Not so much.
REDMAN: I've read references to some of these and wanted to just --
HIROSE: Well of course it would all depend upon what camp you went to because the Army did supply a lot of that stuff, as I understand it anyhow.
REDMAN: I don't know if you're musically inclined at all. I'm not a terribly musically inclined person.
HIROSE: No, I'm not either.
REDMAN: So do you remember -- ?
HIROSE: I do recall that one of my friends -- somehow or other, we got hold of the room next to the chem lab. And I don't know why we got it, but we sort of just got it. The chem lab was right there, and all the experiments. We actually did experiments. But we did it on -- each class would have one or two of the same experiment. Three or four people would do this one experiment. Then three or four others. Not every single individual got to do the same experiment over and over because there was just not enough stuff, chemicals or equipment, to do that. So we did that and we somehow worked ourselves into being the chem labs monitors or guys. We did that. We had lots of fun in there because we did have all that stuff. And we also had a lot of physics lab equipment too.
REDMAN: I suspect there may have been even opportunities for mischief.
HIROSE: Oh, yeah. Mischief was one of our -- one of my buddies was a rocket guy and he know how to make the rocket fuel. He says, "You put this and this and this and this together." He did. And he made rockets. We would go near the fence at night between guard towers and set them off. [laughs] And they would go shiiioooo [makes rocket sound]. Of course, we never got them back. I'm sure the guards were wondering, "What in the world was that?" [laughs] But the follow-up on that. His name was Tomio. He has since passed. He went to Cal, graduated, and went to New Mexico, and became a tracker. You know, they track the ones they really shoot up in the air.
REDMAN: Oh, for rockets?
HIROSE: Real rockets.
REDMAN: So that's sort of a precursor then to his career.
HIROSE: Oh, yes. It was his -- he became his career. He followed all of the rockets that went up in the air. Also the ones that at Cape Canaveral, the ones, what do you call them?
REDMAN: Space shuttles.
HIROSE: Space shuttles and all that kind of stuff. But mostly, he was tracking debris, as far as I can understand. He wasn't supposed to tell anything about it. [laugh] That's all I know.
REDMAN: Do you remember instances of group singing? So singing of maybe traditional Japanese songs or singing of songs in English?
HIROSE: Well, we did have a music chorus club.
REDMAN: Can you talk about that?
HIROSE: Well, I wasn't in it, so I don't know what happened. But there was such a choral group. The churches, there was a Christian church and a Buddhist church, they had choral groups in their organizations, and they sang, I guess, on Sundays and so on.
REDMAN: You'd mentioned that your parents were Buddhist.
HIROSE: Yes.
REDMAN: But then you've also hinted that you've had some involvement in the Congregational Church now in Berkeley. You also suggested to me that you didn't go to Buddhist church with your parents at one point. Can I maybe ask about your religious point of view?
HIROSE: Well, our family, the Hirose family, is a Buddhist family, so I'm a Buddhist. My connection with the Congregational Church is I went there after we got back. This lady in there was very kind to me. She said, "Here's a whole bunch of letters that people from our congregation have sent to internees, and letters from internees back to the congregation." She gave a copy of the whole -- it's a really big, thick book. I organized it by date, earliest ones to latest ones. Then the original letter and the response. It could be either way.
REDMAN: So you helped organized these letters are they were being prepared to be put into a book.
HIROSE: Yes.
REDMAN: Oh, that's tremendous.
HIROSE: And it's not a book, like that. A paperback. It's a -- what do you call those things? CDs.
REDMAN: Do you have anything else to add? I wonder about what kind of music were you listening to in those days, like were you listening to jazz or blues or other forms of popular music?
HIROSE: That's what I was going to get to. In our chem lab, after school was over, we got hold of -- I don't know whose it was or how he got hold of it, but we got hold of a record player, an amplifier, and some speakers, and we would play records of that period.
REDMAN: Do you recall what kind of music?
HIROSE: What?
REDMAN: Examples of that would be great.
HIROSE: Glenn Miller. Arty Shaw. I got a whole bunch of them.
REDMAN: So a lot of swing.
HIROSE: Yeah, swing. Yes. All the big bands of the Big Band era. The popular singer at the time was the little kid. I forgot his name. No --
REDMAN: Oh, that's fine. So we talked a lot about --
HIROSE: And that's what we played in our chem lab. [laughs]
REDMAN: When you could get a hold of it, you would listen to the big band, the fun stuff. Okay. How aware were you guys of the war that was going on?
HIROSE: Oh, we knew that it was going on, but we got no information coming in. We didn't have a radio.
REDMAN: So I understand Manzanar organizes their own newspaper, sort of famously. [Manzanar Free Press]
HIROSE: Yes, they did.
REDMAN: What about Topaz? Was there any sort of newsletter?
HIROSE: Oh, I don't recall seeing a Topaz newsletter. There may have been, but I don't recall.
REDMAN: There're also perceptions, of course, that the Japanese are spying and that there's some sort of spy program. Of course, you've mentioned the restrictions on radios and cameras that I think directly play into these fears of espionage. Did you get that idea that people were thought of as spies automatically for being Japanese?
HIROSE: Yes. We were spies. Automatically I was a spy. I don't know why. I says, "Why would they think I'm a spy? I can't do anything like that. I don't have a radio, I don't have a camera, I don't have anything. Besides, I can't get out of here anyhow!" [laughs]
REDMAN: So that perception, that's definitely something that you felt.
HIROSE: But I think -- yeah, I guess that's just what we felt. That's why we didn't have these things. It was the -- the explanation for us for not having radios and so on is, because potentially they're spy equipment.
REDMAN: Let's talk about the end of the war. What did you hear of the dropping of the atomic weapons on Hiroshima and Nagasaki?
HIROSE: I heard about it way after it happened. Not that day.
REDMAN: Can you tell me about that?
HIROSE: Well, I don't really remember much more of it except "They dropped a what?" You know? An atomic bomb, "What's that?" "Well, it's a big, big, big bomb." That's all I knew, at that time, which is well after it was dropped anyhow. The thing that made big news was the fact that MacArthur was going to have a surrendering ceremony of some sort. "Oh, so the war has come to an end." But the notion of the atomic bomb --
REDMAN: Was so abstract, it sounds like.
HIROSE: Yes. It just didn't make any sense at that time.
REDMAN: Let me jump back just for a moment. This is something I suspect you didn't hear about, or didn't hear about at the time. You probably are familiar with it from living in the Bay Area. In 1944 in June, there was a massive explosion at a naval base called Port Chicago that killed predominantly black servicemen. Did you hear anything about that? I imagine that it would have been completely unknown at the camps.
HIROSE: No. I heard about it afterwards. Even after, there was a student of mine who was in the Navy, but in the Navy Air Force. He came on -- occasionally, to Port Chicago. I said, "Well, what happened?" And then he told me about it. I said, "Oh my god, what happened?" That's when I really got all the details, especially the part where they were loading or unloading ammunition. And the people that were doing it were all black GIs.
REDMAN: Did you, at the end of the war, so then a lot of Japanese, there's this sense of "Where am I going to move? What I going to do?" Many don't go back to the same place. They go back to different locations. Was your family set and ready to go -- ? You talked about how your family had flirted with the idea of moving to Japan. And especially your mother was in favor of that. Talk about the decision at the end of the war to move back to the Bay Area.
HIROSE: At the end of the war, they were going to close camp. And right before they close camp, they sent me, because I was No-No, they sent me to Santa Fe. So I had to go to Santa Fe.
REDMAN: Separate from your family.
HIROSE: Separated from my family. So what happened at home was that they just said, "Okay, we're going back to Berkeley." It was the decision that was made. So they came back to Berkeley. They go to Berkeley, and they stayed at the temple for a while, a couple days as I understand it. But they had to find a place to stay because, you know, people were coming. Well, pretty soon it's going to get full! So everybody had to get out, or get out as soon as they could so the next bunch of people could get in. I was sent to Santa Fe, and I stayed there until, what was it, '46, toward the end and most of -- six or seven months there.
REDMAN: Now would you briefly describe that experience? Was it all No-No Boys in Santa Fe?
HIROSE: Yes. Mostly.
REDMAN: Young men like you?
HIROSE: Yes, people like me. Also, there was, for some reason, I don't know to this day, and maybe you know more about it than I do, but from Peru. Niseis from Peru who were interned --
REDMAN: In Santa Fe.
HIROSE: -- in Santa Fe because they came to the United Stated undocumented. Well, they were undocumented because they were all gathered up by the United States government and brought to the US and put in jail and then put in to Santa Fe. As much as I can gather, they had meetings all over Peru at city halls or something, and they got all the young men. The United States government got all the young men and just took them away.
REDMAN: So was that experience different? To be with only young men rather than families?
HIROSE: Yeah, there's no families. It's just all men in Santa Fe. That's the Immigration-Naturalization Service.
REDMAN: So then you had to be re-naturalized or how did that process go? So then I wonder --
HIROSE: For me, the process was -- I said "No, no." Then I had to renounce my citizenship. So I was no longer a US citizen. I went to Santa Fe, stayed there for a little while, six or seven months, whatever it was, and they says, "Okay, now you can go home." But I was not a citizen. The ACLU picked that up, and they made a class action, is that what they --
REDMAN: Yes, class-action lawsuit.
HIROSE: So all the people who renounced their citizenship could join that. I think the attorney's name was Wayne Collins. I remember that. I think he did it in San Francisco. I'm not sure, but I think that's where he went. And then the whole renunciation was overturned.
REDMAN: Was that your introduction to the JACL? You'd mentioned that before the war, you weren't really aware that there was --
HIROSE: I wasn't aware of them. That was kind of one the things that made me more familiar with the JACL.
REDMAN: And it seems like that would have been a big deal for you, that they --
HIROSE: Oh, yeah.
REDMAN: -- that they advocated for you to get your citizenship back. It seems like that would make me a lifelong supporter of that organization in some sense.
HIROSE: I'm still a contributor to ACLU, yes.
REDMAN: We're getting close to the end of my questions. So let's briefly, let's go through. You were drafted by the US Army.
HIROSE: Yeah.
REDMAN: In '46 or '47.
HIROSE: '47, I think it was.
REDMAN: And then you're in the Army for a couple years.
HIROSE: Yes, I was in the Army for a couple years. And I went to Germany.
REDMAN: Oh, really? What was it like to be a Japanese American in the Army only shortly after the end of the war in Germany?
HIROSE: Well [laughs] --
REDMAN: [laughs] It's a big question.
HIROSE: I just wore my uniform, and that's it. So it was fine. I was just like any other GI?
REDMAN: So you felt like there wasn't discrimination in terms of --
HIROSE: No.
REDMAN: Now I want to ask about modern day stuff, but the ACLU brings me to a good question, which is your feelings on what builds as the redress movement in the seventies and eighties and nineties to symbolically in some respects, financially in other respects, offer official government apologies for what happened to the Japanese and Japanese Americans during World War II. What are your feelings on the redress movement as it played out later on?
HIROSE: Well, I know that the JACL was all involved in that, and they were pushing for it. I said, "Oh, well that's good. I wonder when it's going to happen. It'll probably take another ten more years or something." And all of a sudden, it happened, and who was it signed? Bush, wasn't it? Yeah, Bush signed the letter. And then the appropriation came after that. And it was all distributed.
REDMAN: So did that make you feel anything in particular in terms of --
HIROSE: No.
REDMAN: So it didn't bother you but it didn't --
HIROSE: No, it didn't bother me at all that they did that. I said, "Oh, that's good!" I made use of my funds to go to school because after I got out of the Army, I went to school. And that's what I used it all up for, for that.
REDMAN: You came to UC Berkeley after --
HIROSE: Yes.
REDMAN: So what year did you start at Cal?
HIROSE: Well, let's see. I got out in '48. So in the fall of '48, I think it was? Yes. I started here somewhere.
REDMAN: [laughs] Somewhere around this little spot [on campus].
HIROSE: The only thing -- I don't know, this place is so new.
REDMAN: Well that's a great point that so much had changed over World War II in the Bay Area because of the influx of people that came here for the shipyard industry and stuff like that. Just briefly, did you see a lot of those changes? I wonder if maybe that played in a little bit to your family struggling to find housing at first.
