HIROSE: Well, my dad, I don't know how he did it but he was able to find a house in '46, because that's when the camps closed. By '47 he was able to find a house. I don't know where he got the money.
REDMAN: Did it seem like you came back to the same old Berkeley?
HIROSE: Same place. But there were just more people, it seemed like. There were more people. That's all. But Cal seemed like -- oh, there's a lot of students. But they weren't young. They were all older guys.
REDMAN: That was a common thread was that you as GI would have come back; you were a little older. One of things that would have dawned on me in your generation, and I suspect it would have been a different factor for women in some respects than for men, but you would have returned as an older student, and also a Japanese American having spent time in camp, and then also having been in the service, and all that that life entails. Being a student for you, you must not have felt like your typical college freshman in those days.
HIROSE: I don't think so. [laughs] There were some freshman who were eighteen years old. "Well, they're doing what they're doing and I'm doing what they're doing, so I guess that's the way it is. Just make the best of it! And have fun!"
REDMAN: My name is Sam Redman. It is August 30, 2012, and this is my third tape today with Norman Hirose. When we were in the interim, there was something that came to mind about life at Topaz which was that you actually had a job as a young man at Topaz. What was your job?
HIROSE: Well, we moved to Topaz in '42, toward the end of '42. And then I went through the tenth, eleventh, and twelfth, then graduated. So that means '43. '44. '45. Forty-six is when the camp closed, so I was in school for actually two years. Sort of skipped the tenth grade.
REDMAN: Were there graduation ceremonies?
HIROSE: Oh, we had graduation ceremonies. Caps and gowns and everything. Even Pomp and Circumstance. Of course, it was a recording but then it was there.
REDMAN: And your job?
HIROSE: Towards the middle of the senior year, I was wandering around the hospital for some reason or other. A friend of mine says, "You want to work in a warehouse?" and I says, "Sure, I'll work anywhere. It doesn't matter." So I worked in the warehouse. We would gather up all of the requests from the different wards. The men's ward wanted this, the pediatric ward wants this, the OB ward wants so and so. We get all that stuff and cart it down. Then one day the Head Nurse saw me and says, "Norman, do you want to transfer over to the surgery?" I says, "Why?" "Well, we need somebody in there to clean up the place." [laughs] I says, "Okay!" So I left warehouse and went to Surgery. What it meant was -- after surgery, it's pretty messy. So it needs to be cleaned up, and all of the things need to be washed, and all that. We didn't have to do that; we just had to prepare them for the laundry. Then we had to prepare instruments and all that kind for the next operation, whatever it's going to be. For any emergency that might come up.
REDMAN: Norman, this sounds like a dirty job. [laughs]
HIROSE: It was. At times, it was messy because it was really bloody because after surgery. But after we got all the laundry out of the way, we got all the instruments cleaned, and then sterilized the instruments, got them all ready for the next operation. Then of course, we had to clean the whole surgical room itself.
REDMAN: What was your impression of the medical care that people got at that hospital?
HIROSE: Oh, our hospital was pretty good. Very good. The guy in-charge was from outside; I forgot his name. The three doctors that were inside were from -- the one guy, Dr. Goto from USC, and Dr. Sugiyama from Northwestern, and Dr. Teshima was from UCSF.
REDMAN: So that's a pretty highly educated --
HIROSE: Yes, so they're --
REDMAN: -- bunch.
HIROSE: And they were already practicing doctors. They weren't interning or anything like that. So they came. And they were our doctors. But I think they distributed the doctors that way throughout all of the ten different camps.
REDMAN: I see.
HIROSE: If one camp had too many doctors and they --
REDMAN: Sent the family to another one. Let's talk about how all of these things are remembered. Actually, first, let me just ask about Berkeley really quickly. When you were here, did you get involved at all with the International House?
HIROSE: No. I knew the International House was up on the hill, but I never went there. I went right over here to Stephens Union.
REDMAN: When that was -- ?
HIROSE: Now, Stephens is the building right over there? You know where it is?
REDMAN: Yes, that's right. Yes
HIROSE: We played bridge upstairs. [laughs] Should have been in class, but --
REDMAN: I wonder about -- there was a man named Allen Blaisdell who was the Executive Director of the International House. That name doesn't --
HIROSE: No.
REDMAN: Okay, that's fine. I wonder if you could talk just a little bit -- we've talked around it a little. Could you tell me about your feelings or your ideas up towards Japanese internment histories, commemorations, memorials? Thinking about television documentaries or Ansel Adams photographs or Dorothea Lange photographs, and the many books that have been written about this experience since that time. How do you think it's remembered?
HIROSE: There's a lot of books out there. And I say, "Oh, this guy wrote another book, this guy wrote another book." I haven't read any of them, though, so I don't really know. But I know Harry Kitano. Have you read him? He is from the San Francisco Bay Area, but he got his doctorate in UCLA or somewhere down there or USC, anyhow in the south. And he became a sociologist and wrote a lot of books on sociology about the internment system.
REDMAN: How about the creation of historical landmarks and other programs via the National Park Service. What do you think of those efforts?
HIROSE: I think it's nice to have a marker or something like that. And if people want to go back on a pilgrimage, they better go now for the next ten years, because after that there won't be anybody alive that went, you know, like Heart Mountain, Wyoming. I don't think there's anybody alive that was interned there. Topaz, there's some. I'm one of them. [laughs]
REDMAN: So now, here's my final question. When you think back -- and this is the biggest one and always it's the hardest one -- when you think back in those years in your life, what comes to mind for you?
HIROSE: Well, I think -- Now, I know I get my head chopped off for it but that's okay. I think it was a good thing that happened. And the reason I say it's good is because prior to that time, prior to World War II, Japanese were all right here. Right along the coast, maybe some -- a few in Oregon, and a few in Washington. And that was it. But now you can go almost anywhere in the United States and you'll find Nisei or Sansei now living their lives and just melded in with the rest of the country. And I think that's the greatest thing that happened.
REDMAN: So some unintentional positive consequences could have emerged.
HIROSE: That's it. That's the single most important thing to me that happened. I really think that if it wasn't that way, we'd still be just living up and down here.
REDMAN: Is there anything else you'd like to add?
HIROSE: Well, for me the whole internment thing that happened -- of course, it turned out so well that I have no complaints about it. It was a great big adventure for me. People will say, "How can it have been an adventure? It was so bad." Well, it wasn't that bad. In fact, half of it was fun for me. Afterwards, it wasn't so bad. We had to start all over again. But my sister grew up; she got married. My brother grew up and he got -- I grew up, and I got married too. So I don't know what's wrong with that. It was an adventure, I think. There are some people who were very bitter, and I feel sorry for them. They're bitter, and their lives were, in a way, wasted on being bitter. And that's just sad.
REDMAN: With that, I'd like to say thank you so much for sitting down and joining us today.
HIROSE: No, it's been fun for me.
REDMAN: Thank you.
[End of interview]
